title: On-Call & Incident Management at FAANG
description: How on-call, incident response, and postmortems work at FAANG — a significant culture shock for SAP engineers used to SAP Solution Manager and transport-driven operations. Covers severity levels, incident command structure, blameless postmortems, SLO/error budgets, on-call rotations, and how to prepare for the on-call reality as a Staff+ engineer.

sections.title: On-Call at FAANG vs SAP Operations

description.value:
In SAP environments, operations are handled by a separate Basis team. Developers write code, transport it to production, and the Basis team monitors and maintains the systems. If something breaks at 3 AM, the Basis team pages their on-call engineer, not the ABAP developer who wrote the code. FAANG operates on a completely different philosophy: you build it, you run it.

At FAANG, the engineer who writes the code is responsible for operating it in production. Every engineer participates in an on-call rotation. When your service is paged at 3 AM, you are the first responder. Not a separate operations team. You. This is the single biggest operational culture shock for SAP engineers, and it's non-negotiable at every FAANG company.

Key differences: SAP uses planned maintenance windows (downtime is scheduled and communicated weeks in advance). FAANG deploys continuously and expects zero-downtime upgrades. SAP has a formal change advisory board (CAB) that approves changes. FAANG uses peer review and automated deployment pipelines. SAP monitors with Solution Manager (centralized, ITIL-based). FAANG uses distributed observability stacks (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog) with alerting rules owned by each service team.

Why FAANG does this: When engineers are on-call for their own code, they write more resilient code, add better monitoring, and fix reliability issues proactively. The on-call pager is the ultimate code review. It catches what automated tests and peer reviews miss. SAP's separation of development and operations means developers never feel the pain of their own design decisions in production.

sections.title: Severity Levels and Incident Response Structure

description.value:
FAANG companies use a severity classification system for incidents. While the exact names differ (Google uses S0-S4, Amazon uses Sev1-Sev5, Meta uses P0-P4), the structure is consistent. Understanding this structure is essential for your first on-call shift.

Sev1 (Critical): Customer-facing outage affecting all users or major functionality. Example: checkout page returns 500 errors for all users. Response: 5-minute acknowledgment target, incident commander appointed immediately, all-hands-on-deck, executive notification within 15 minutes. Postmortem required within 48 hours. SAP equivalent: production system down during month-end close for all company codes.

Sev2 (Major): Partial degradation or outage affecting a subset of users. Example: payment processing delayed by 30 minutes but not failing. Response: 15-minute acknowledgment, on-call engineer leads investigation, notify team. Postmortem may be required depending on impact. SAP equivalent: a specific BAPI is slow but not failing.

Sev3 (Minor): Minor issue with workaround available. Example: error message not displaying correctly in the UI. Response: next business day, tracked as a bug. No postmortem. SAP equivalent: a custom Z-report shows incorrect formatting.

Sev4 (Low): Cosmetic issue, feature request, or documentation fix. Response: handled through normal backlog. SAP equivalent: transport request with minor UI label changes.

The Incident Command System (ICS): For Sev1 incidents, an incident commander is appointed. Their job is NOT to debug the technical issue, but to coordinate the response: track timeline, assign tasks, communicate status to stakeholders, and decide when to escalate. The incident commander is typically a Staff+ engineer with experience running incidents. The actual debugging is done by Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) who focus entirely on the technical problem while the commander manages everything else.

# Incident Response Runbook Template
# Team: [Service Name]
# On-Call Contact: [Phone/Pager]

# S1 - Critical Outage
# Trigger: PagerDuty alert, 5-min acknowledgment
# Response:
#   1. ACKNOWLEDGE incident in PagerDuty
#   2. JOIN incident Slack channel #inc-[service]-[date]
#   3. ASSESS: Is this a Sev1? (all users affected, revenue impact, data loss)
#   4. DECLARE incident in incident management tool
#   5. APPOINT Incident Commander (most senior available)
#   6. NOTIFY: Manager, Director-on-call, SRE team via Slack
#   7. MITIGATE: Rollback, feature flag off, or hotfix
#   8. POSTMORTEM: Schedule within 48 hours

# S2 - Partial Degradation
# Trigger: PagerDuty alert or manual report
# Response:
#   1. ACKNOWLEDGE within 15 min
#   2. INVESTIGATE root cause
#   3. FIX or escalate to S1 if scope grows
#   4. POSTMORTEM: Optional, depends on impact

# S3 - Minor Issue
# Response: Next business day, file bug

# Key Contacts for Escalation:
# Manager: [Name]
# Sr. Engineer: [Name]
# SRE Team: #sre-oncall
# Director On-Call: [Name]

sections.title: Blameless Postmortems — FAANG's Most Important Operational Practice

description.value:
After every Sev1 incident (and many Sev2s), FAANG companies require a written postmortem within 48 hours. The postmortem is the most important operational artifact you will produce as a Staff+ engineer. It's not a blame document — it's a learning document. The fundamental principle: blamelessness. The system failed, not the person. The question is not 'who made the mistake?' but 'what in the system allowed the mistake to happen?'

Postmortem structure: Summary (one paragraph describing the incident and impact). Timeline (exact timestamps of every event: alert triggered, acknowledged, investigated, mitigated, resolved). Contributing Factors (the technical and process factors that enabled the incident). Mitigation Steps (what was done to resolve it). Action Items (specific, assigned, tracked changes to prevent recurrence, each with an owner and deadline). Lessons Learned (what surprised the team, what monitoring gaps were exposed, what would you do differently).

SAP contrast: SAP culture often blames the developer who created a bad transport ('who moved this to production without testing?'). FAANG culture blames the process that didn't catch the bad transport ('why doesn't our staging environment catch this type of error?'). The difference is profound: blame leads to hiding mistakes; blamelessness leads to fixing root causes. As a Staff+ engineer, you set the tone for blamelessness. When an incident happens on your watch, your first question should be 'what can we learn?' not 'who did this?'

Writing good action items: Bad action item: 'Write better tests.' Good action item: 'Add integration test for payment reconciliation flow covering null merchant_id edge case, by Feb 15, owned by [name].' Bad action item: 'Improve monitoring.' Good action item: 'Add Grafana alert for p99 latency > 500ms on the payment-processing service, by Feb 20, owned by [name].' Each action item must be specific, measurable, and tracked to completion.

Summary (one paragraph, what happened and impact)</text><text class="pm-sub" x="350" y="233">2. Timeline (exact timestamps of every event)</text><text class="pm-sub" x="350" y="251">3. Contributing Factors (why did this happen?)</text><text class="pm-sub" x="350" y="269">4. Action Items (specific, assigned, tracked)</text><text class="pm-sub" x="350" y="287">5. Lessons Learned (surprises, gaps, improvements)</text><text class="pm-sub" x="350" y="305" font-style="italic">Staff+ engineers set the tone: always ask 'what can we learn?' not 'who did this?'</text></svg>

Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are the language of reliability at FAANG. As a Staff+ engineer, you define and own the SLOs for your systems. This is a significant responsibility that doesn't exist in SAP environments (where SLA is contractual and managed by Basis).

Service Level Indicator (SLI): A quantifiable metric that measures a specific aspect of service behavior. Common SLIs: availability (percentage of successful requests), latency (p50, p95, p99 response times), throughput (requests per second), error rate (percentage of failed requests), durability (for storage systems). For an SAP integration hub, your SLIs might include: message delivery success rate, end-to-end latency for financial transactions, number of failed retries.

Service Level Objective (SLO): A target value for a specific SLI over a period. Example: '99.9% of messages delivered successfully within 5 seconds, measured over a 30-day rolling window.' The SLO is your commitment to your stakeholders. It's not aspirational — it's the threshold that triggers action when breached.

Error Budget: 100% minus your SLO. If your SLO is 99.9% availability, your error budget is 0.1% (approximately 8.7 hours of downtime per year per service). As long as you have error budget remaining, you can deploy new features, refactor code, or make risky changes. When you exhaust your error budget, you STOP shipping features and invest in reliability instead. This is the mechanism that balances feature velocity and reliability without requiring management intervention.

SAP context: In SAP, there is no error budget. The Basis team promises 99.95% uptime (contractual SLA) and everyone expects it to be met regardless of how many changes are shipped. At FAANG, SLOs and error budgets make the tradeoff explicit: if you want to ship faster, you accept more risk. If you want higher reliability, you ship slower. Staff+ engineers make this tradeoff decision for their teams.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: SLO Target Selection for an SAP Integration Hub

variables.name: eng_cost_reliability
variables.description: Engineering cost to improve from 99.9% to 99.95%

variables.name: cost_per_minute_downtime
variables.description: Estimated cost per minute of integration downtime

variables.name: feature_velocity_loss
variables.description: Feature velocity lost if 6 eng-months spent on reliability

calculation.formula: Error budget at 99.9% = 43 min/month. At 99.95% = 21 min/month. Cost of improving SLO: 6 eng-months ($500K). Value of reduced downtime = (43-21) × 12 min × $12K = $3.2M/year. But: lost feature velocity = $1.5M in delayed revenue.
calculation.cp: Higher SLO (99.95%): $500K investment + $1.5M delayed features = $2M cost. Savings from reduced downtime: $3.2M/year.
calculation.ap: Lower SLO (99.9%): accept 22 more minutes of downtime/month = $264K/year in downtime costs. Zero investment and no delayed features.
calculation.breakeven: Net benefit of higher SLO: $3.2M - $2M = $1.2M in year 1, then $3.2M/year ongoing. However, if feature velocity loss delays a revenue-critical feature, the breakeven changes. Staff+ decision: choose 99.9% for now, improve to 99.95% after the critical feature ships.

sections.title: On-Call Rotation and Expectations for Staff+

description.value:
On-call rotations at FAANG follow a standard pattern: primary on-call (first responder for all alerts), secondary on-call (backup for the primary), and escalation engineer (Staff+ engineer who handles Sev1 incidents and mentors the primary). Typical rotation frequency: one week primary, one week secondary, every 4-8 weeks depending on team size.

Staff+ on-call responsibilities: As a Staff+ engineer, your on-call role differs from an L4/L5 engineer. You should NOT be primary on-call every rotation (that's not how the role scales). Instead, you are typically the escalation engineer — you get called when the primary can't resolve a Sev1 or when the incident commander needs technical leadership. Your job during on-call is not just to fix the immediate issue but to identify systemic fixes that prevent the issue from recurring.

On-call preparation for your first rotation: (1) Read the last 2 months of postmortems for your service. (2) Read the on-call runbook (if it doesn't exist, your first on-call contribution should be writing it). (3) Shadow the current primary for 3-4 days before your rotation starts. (4) Set up your monitoring dashboards and alerting channels beforehand. (5) Know the escalation path: who do you call if you can't resolve?

SAP-specific on-call advice: Your first on-call rotation will be terrifying. You don't know the codebase, the monitoring tools, or the alerting rules. This is normal. Survive it by: (1) over-communicating in the incident channel ('I'm investigating, will update in 5 minutes'), (2) escalating early if you're stuck (don't spend 2 hours debugging something you don't understand), (3) taking detailed notes for the postmortem (your fresh perspective is valuable), (4) asking for a debrief after your rotation ends. Every engineer's first on-call rotation is a disaster. The second one is better. By the third, you know the system.

adversarialResponse.title: Handling the 'I'm Not Operations' Objection
adversarialResponse.challenge: You join FAANG as a Staff+ engineer from SAP. When told about the on-call rotation, you think: 'I'm a Staff engineer, not an SRE. I shouldn't be woken up at 3 AM for operational issues.' This mindset will fail at FAANG.
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Complain to your manager that on-call is beneath your level, avoid being paged, or delegate your on-call responsibilities to junior engineers. This signals that you don't embrace operational excellence — a core FAANG value. You'll be perceived as entitled and will struggle to get promoted.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Embrace on-call as the fastest learning opportunity available. On your first rotation, you learn: (1) every system boundary and failure mode, (2) which parts of the system are fragile, (3) which monitoring is missing, (4) which documentation is inadequate. After 2-3 rotations, you'll know the system better than engineers who've been there a year. Use this knowledge to: improve runbooks, add monitoring, fix reliability issues, and mentor junior engineers on incident response. Staff+ who excel at on-call build the deepest system knowledge and earn the most trust.
adversarialResponse.pattern: The 'learning opportunity' reframe: On-call isn't a tax. It's an investment that pays the highest dividend in system knowledge. At Staff+ level, you're not just fixing the current incident — you're improving the entire incident response system. Write postmortems, improve runbooks, reduce alert fatigue. That's the Staff+ multiplier effect.

sections.title: Improving the On-Call Experience as a Staff+ Engineer

description.value:
After you've been through 2-3 on-call rotations, you have a responsibility to improve the on-call experience for everyone. This is one of the highest-leverage activities for a Staff+ engineer. The goal: reduce alert fatigue, improve runbooks, automate remediation, and make on-call sustainable for the entire team.

Reduce alert fatigue: Every alert that fires at 3 AM should represent a real customer-facing issue. If it doesn't, either fix the alert threshold or remove it. Common problems: alerts for p99 latency spikes that self-resolve in 2 minutes (increase the evaluation window), alerts for errors that are expected during deployments (silence during deployment windows), alerts for known issues with no action (acknowledge and file a bug instead of alerting).

Improve runbooks: After each incident, update the runbook with what you learned. The first time you encounter a new error, document the debugging steps. The second time, create a script that automates the diagnosis. The third time, fix the root cause so the error never happens again. This is the progression from reactive to proactive operations.

Automate common remediations: If the same type of incident happens twice, automate the fix. Common automation targets: restarting a service that's in a bad state (add health check with auto-recovery), clearing a stuck queue (add dead-letter processing), scaling a service under load (add autoscaling).

Build the on-call culture: As a Staff+ engineer, you set the cultural norms for on-call. Model the behavior you want to see: acknowledge alerts promptly, communicate clearly during incidents, write thorough postmortems, thank people for catching issues. When you build a culture where on-call is respected and supported, the entire team's operational excellence improves.

details: Staff+ Insight: On-call is the single biggest culture shock for SAP engineers joining FAANG. But it's also the fastest way to learn a new codebase and build technical credibility. Volunteer for on-call early in your tenure — your first 2 rotations will teach you more about the system than 6 months of feature development would. The best Staff+ engineers are those who have been paged at 3 AM, know what keeps them up at night, and systematically eliminate those pages through better engineering. Your SAP operational experience (runbooks, escalation procedures, change management) is actually a superpower in on-call — use it to build better operational practices for your team.
